greeting: Hey! I'm Kairu. CAPE Biology Unit 1 deepens what you saw at CSEC — biomolecules, cells, membranes, energetics, genetics, and reproduction. The maths is heavier (rates, ratios, table reading) so read every option carefully. Cross-check answers you flag in red with your teacher.

questions.stem: The diagram shows two biological molecules: Molecule I has a backbone with three amino-acid-like residues joined by C-N linkages; Molecule II shows two ring sugar residues joined by an oxygen bridge. Which of the following combinations correctly describes the structure of Molecules I and II?

options.A: 2 amino acids joined by a peptide bond; 2 alpha-glucose residues joined by a 1,4 glycosidic bond
options.B: 2 amino acids joined by a glycosidic bond; 2 beta-glucose residues joined by a 1,4 peptide bond
options.C: 3 amino acids joined by 2 peptide bonds; 2 beta-glucose residues joined by a 1,4 glycosidic bond
options.D: 3 amino acids joined by 2 glycosidic bonds; 2 alpha-glucose residues joined by a peptide bond

questions.explanation: Molecule I shows 3 amino acids linked by 2 peptide bonds (a tripeptide). Molecule II shows two beta-glucose residues joined by a 1,4 glycosidic bond, typical of cellulose.

distractors.A: Only describes two amino acids; the diagram clearly shows three residues with two peptide bonds.
distractors.B: Confuses bond names: amino acids form peptide bonds, not glycosidic, and sugars form glycosidic bonds.
distractors.D: Glycosidic bonds do not join amino acids; peptide bonds do not join sugars.

questions.stem: Carbohydrates are transported in plants MAINLY in the form of
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are translocated through the phloem primarily as sucrose, a non-reducing disaccharide that is soluble and chemically stable.

distractors.A: Starch is a storage polysaccharide; it is insoluble and not transported.
distractors.C: Glucose is used in respiration and converted to sucrose for transport; it is too reactive to be transported in bulk.
distractors.D: Fructose is not the main transport sugar; it is produced when sucrose is hydrolysed.

questions.stem: The diagram shows two isomers of glucose. Isomer I has the -OH on carbon 1 in the alpha (down) position, and Isomer II has the -OH on carbon 1 in the beta (up) position. Which of the following statements is true about BOTH isomers?

options.A: Both contribute to the structure of starch.
options.B: Both contribute to the structure of cellulose.
options.C: Structure I contributes to the structure of cellulose and Structure II contributes to the structure of starch.
options.D: Structure I contributes to the structure of starch and Structure II contributes to the structure of cellulose.

questions.explanation: Alpha-glucose (Isomer I) is the monomer of starch, while beta-glucose (Isomer II) is the monomer of cellulose.

distractors.A: Only alpha-glucose forms starch.
distractors.B: Only beta-glucose forms cellulose.
distractors.C: The roles are reversed.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST represents the structure of a triglyceride? (A) A phosphate-sugar-base nucleotide structure. (B) Three sugar rings linked by glycosidic bonds (a polysaccharide). (C) A glycerol backbone with three fatty acid chains (R, R', R'') attached by ester bonds. (D) A glycerol with two fatty acid chains and a phosphate group (a phospholipid).
questions.explanation: A triglyceride is formed from one glycerol and three fatty acids joined by ester bonds, releasing three molecules of water during condensation.

distractors.A: This is a nucleotide, not a lipid.
distractors.B: This is a polysaccharide.
distractors.D: This describes a phospholipid (two fatty acids + phosphate).

questions.stem: Collagen exhibits a secondary level of protein structure and organization because

options.B: each chain forms a helix held by covalent bonds
options.C: each chain forms a helix held by hydrogen bonds
options.D: it is made up of repeated units of three different amino acids

questions.explanation: Secondary structure in proteins is defined by the coiling/folding of the polypeptide into helices or sheets stabilised by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms. In collagen each chain forms a left-handed helix held by hydrogen bonds.

distractors.A: Having four chains describes quaternary structure.
distractors.B: Secondary structure is held by hydrogen bonds, not covalent bonds.
distractors.D: Sequence of amino acids describes primary structure.

questions.stem: When a mixture of starch and amylase is tested with a solution of iodine in potassium iodide, a blue-black colour is initially observed. However, when tested after one minute, the blue-black colour is no longer observed. This is due to the formation of
questions.explanation: Amylase hydrolyses starch into the disaccharide maltose. Once starch has been broken down, it no longer reacts with iodine to give the blue-black colour.

distractors.A: Sucrose is not a product of amylase digestion.
distractors.C: Cellulose is a polysaccharide, not a product of starch digestion.
distractors.D: Glycogen is an animal storage polysaccharide, not produced by amylase.

questions.stem: The diagram shows four bonds. Which of the following occurs in the tertiary structure but NOT in the primary structure of a protein? (A) A peptide bond between -C(=O)- and -N(H)-. (B) A hydrogen bond between two amino acid side chains. (C) An ester linkage H-C-O-C-R. (D) A disulphide bridge between two amino acids in the side chain.
questions.explanation: Tertiary structure involves bonds between R-groups, especially disulphide bridges between cysteine residues. Primary structure only involves peptide bonds between amino acids in sequence.

distractors.A: Peptide bonds occur in primary structure.
distractors.B: Hydrogen bonds can occur in secondary, tertiary and quaternary structure, but are not the most specific tertiary feature here.
distractors.C: Ester linkages are not found in proteins.

questions.stem: Which of the following combinations correctly identifies structures typical of a eukaryotic cell? (Key: tick = present, cross = absent across columns Endoplasmic Reticulum, Mitochondria, Nuclear Membrane, 70S Ribosome.)

options.A: ER present, mitochondria present, nuclear membrane present, 70S ribosome present
options.B: ER present, mitochondria present, nuclear membrane present, 70S ribosome absent
options.C: ER absent, mitochondria present, nuclear membrane absent, 70S ribosome present
options.D: ER absent, mitochondria absent, nuclear membrane absent, 70S ribosome present

questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cells have ER, mitochondria and a nuclear membrane, but contain 80S (not 70S) ribosomes in the cytoplasm. 70S ribosomes are characteristic of prokaryotes.

distractors.A: Eukaryotes do not typically have 70S ribosomes in the cytoplasm.
distractors.C: Eukaryotes have ER and a nuclear membrane.
distractors.D: Eukaryotes have mitochondria and a nuclear membrane.

questions.stem: The diagram shows a section of a cell under an electron microscope with region I labelled on the nuclear membrane (a nuclear pore). Which of the following statements BEST explains the function of the region labelled I?

options.A: It allows for the transport of small DNA fragments into the cytoplasm.
options.B: It contains protein channels for the active transport of water-soluble molecules.
options.C: It contains protein channels which restrict the movement of water molecules.
options.D: It allows for the transport of mRNA and ribosomal components into the cytoplasm.

questions.explanation: Nuclear pores are large protein-lined channels that allow mRNA and ribosomal subunits to move from the nucleus into the cytoplasm.

distractors.A: DNA fragments are not normally exported through nuclear pores.
distractors.B: Transport through nuclear pores is selective but not classically described as active in CSEC syllabus.
distractors.C: Nuclear pores do not restrict water movement.

questions.stem: Which of the following combinations forms evidence which BEST supports the endosymbiont theory?

options.A: Prokaryotic cells contain circular DNA; eukaryotic cells contain linear DNA in mitochondria
options.B: Prokaryotic cells contain circular DNA; eukaryotic cells contain circular DNA in mitochondria
options.C: Prokaryotic cells contain circular DNA in mitochondria; eukaryotic cells contain linear DNA in mitochondria
options.D: Prokaryotic cells contain linear DNA in mitochondria; eukaryotic cells contain circular DNA in mitochondria

questions.explanation: The endosymbiont theory proposes that mitochondria evolved from a smaller prokaryote engulfed by a larger prokaryote. Both prokaryotic cells and mitochondria contain circular DNA, supporting this common origin.

distractors.A: Mitochondria contain circular DNA, not linear.
distractors.C: Prokaryotic cells do not have mitochondria.
distractors.D: Mitochondrial DNA is circular, not linear.

questions.stem: Bobby is given a tropical freshwater fish for his birthday. Experimenting with the fish, he places it in seawater and it dies. Which of the following statements MOST likely explains the reason for its death?

options.B: Water diffuses out of the fish.
options.C: Ions diffuse from the fish to the seawater.
options.D: Less oxygen is in seawater than freshwater.

questions.explanation: Answer is B. Worked explanation pending teacher rewrite.

distractors.A: Water moves out, not in, because seawater is more concentrated.
distractors.C: Ions would diffuse INTO the fish from seawater, not out.
distractors.D: Oxygen levels are not the immediate cause of death in this short-term experiment.

questions.stem: A student's plan drawing of the transverse section of a dicotyledonous stem seen under low power shows the drawing measuring 50 mm. The actual diameter of the stem is 10 mm. What is the magnification of the drawing?
questions.explanation: Magnification = image size / actual size = 50 mm / 10 mm = x 5.

distractors.B: x 10 would require an image of 100 mm.
distractors.C: x 100 would require an image of 1000 mm.
distractors.D: x 500 is far larger than the calculation gives.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of endocytosis?

options.A: Engulfing of a bacterium by a phagocyte
options.B: Release of acid into the stomach of a mammal
options.C: Release of water from the contractile vacuole in amoeba
options.D: Formation of Golgi vesicles by budding from cisternae

questions.explanation: Endocytosis is the bulk uptake of material into a cell by enclosing it in a vesicle. Phagocytosis of a bacterium is a classic example.

distractors.B: Acid release is secretion, a form of exocytosis.
distractors.C: Water release from a contractile vacuole is exocytosis.
distractors.D: Vesicle formation from Golgi is internal trafficking, not endocytosis.

questions.stem: The diagram shows a phospholipid bilayer with four labelled structures: (A) a transmembrane channel/protein, (B) a phospholipid head, (C) a cholesterol-like molecule, and (D) a glycoprotein/glycolipid with an attached carbohydrate chain on the outer surface. Which of the labelled structures is important for cell to cell recognition?
questions.explanation: Glycoproteins and glycolipids with carbohydrate chains projecting from the outer surface of the membrane act as cell-surface markers for cell-to-cell recognition.

distractors.A: Channel proteins transport ions, not recognition.
distractors.B: Phospholipid heads form the membrane structure.
distractors.C: Cholesterol stabilises fluidity but is not a recognition marker.

questions.stem: A graph shows the rate of an enzyme-catalysed reaction rising with temperature to an optimum near 35 degrees Celsius, then dropping sharply (point X is on the falling portion). The decline in the rate at X is MOST likely because

options.B: the enzyme loses its secondary structure
options.C: the enzyme loses its tertiary structure

questions.explanation: Above the optimum temperature, enzymes undergo denaturation: the tertiary structure (3-D folding of R-group bonds) is disrupted, so the active site changes shape and substrates cannot bind.

distractors.A: Peptide bonds (primary structure) are not broken by mild heating.
distractors.B: Secondary structure changes are less specific; the loss of active site shape is tertiary.
distractors.D: Substrate concentration is independent of temperature.

questions.stem: The diagram shows four molecules: I ribose sugar, II deoxyribose sugar, III uracil (RNA), IV thymine (DNA). Which two molecules combined with a phosphate group form an RNA nucleotide?
questions.explanation: RNA nucleotides contain the sugar ribose (I) and (when transcribed in place of thymine) the base uracil (III), plus a phosphate group.

distractors.B: Thymine is found in DNA, not RNA.
distractors.C: Deoxyribose is the sugar in DNA, not RNA.
distractors.D: Both deoxyribose and thymine are DNA components.

questions.stem: The diagram shows the relative amount of DNA per nucleus during animal cell division. The DNA level rises (region X) then falls in steps (region Y). Which of the following processes are occurring at X and Y?
questions.explanation: At X, DNA content doubles (2n to 4n) due to DNA synthesis (S phase). At Y, DNA content halves due to separation of chromosomes at mitosis/meiosis.

distractors.B: Roles are reversed.
distractors.C: DNA is not synthesised when amount halves.
distractors.D: DNA must be synthesised before it can be separated.

questions.stem: The diagram shows three stages of mitosis: Stage 1 with chromosomes aligned at the equator; Stage 2 with chromosomes condensed but scattered (early); Stage 3 with chromatids being pulled to opposite poles. Which of the following combinations correctly identifies the stages labelled 1, 2 and 3?
questions.explanation: Stage 1 (alignment at equator) = metaphase; Stage 2 (chromosomes condensing/visible) = prophase; Stage 3 (chromatids separating to poles) = anaphase.

distractors.A: Order does not match the diagrams.
distractors.B: Stage 1 is metaphase, not anaphase.
distractors.D: Stage 2 is not anaphase.

questions.stem: One role of transfer RNA in protein synthesis is to

options.B: terminate the elongation of the polypeptide chain
options.C: act as a supporting framework for polypeptide chains
options.D: carry protein-building instructions from DNA into the cytoplasm

questions.explanation: tRNA binds specific amino acids and delivers them to the ribosome, where they are added to the growing polypeptide chain according to the mRNA codon sequence.

distractors.B: Termination is the role of release factors at stop codons.
distractors.C: tRNA is not a structural framework.
distractors.D: mRNA carries instructions from DNA to the cytoplasm.

questions.stem: In a rat cell, there are 32 chromatids present at the beginning of prophase of mitosis. How many chromatids would there be in the cell at the beginning of Prophase 1 of meiosis?
questions.explanation: DNA replication occurs during interphase BEFORE prophase in both mitosis and meiosis. So the chromatid count at the start of Prophase 1 of meiosis is the same as the chromatid count at the start of prophase of mitosis: 32.

distractors.A: 16 would be the haploid chromatid count after meiosis is complete.
distractors.B: 23 is the human haploid chromosome number, unrelated.
distractors.D: 64 would require two rounds of replication without division.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT true of homologous chromosomes?

options.C: They contain the same number of genes.
options.D: Euchromatin and heterochromatin bands are the same.

questions.explanation: Homologous chromosomes carry the same genes in the same loci, but the alleles (versions of a gene) can be different — one may be inherited from each parent. The other features are common to homologues.

distractors.A: True - homologues have the same length.
distractors.C: True - they carry the same number of genes.
distractors.D: True - banding patterns are matched.

questions.stem: A piece of DNA strand shown is 5' ATAGTATTTCGAAAGAGA 3'. Using the codon table provided, which of the following represents the sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain from this DNA strand?

options.A: Serine - Lysine - Histidine - Phenylalanine - Glycine - Tyrosine
options.B: Serine - Phenylalanine - Alanine - Lysine - Histidine - Tyrosine
options.C: Tyrosine - Glycine - Phenylalanine - Histidine - Lysine - Serine
options.D: Tyrosine - Histidine - Lysine - Alanine - Phenylalanine - Serine

questions.explanation: The DNA strand acts as a template; the mRNA is read in groups of three to give the amino-acid sequence. The matching sequence from this strand is Tyr - His - Lys - Ala - Phe - Ser.

distractors.A: Wrong amino acids and order.
distractors.B: Wrong sequence direction.
distractors.C: Order is reversed from the correct reading frame.

questions.stem: Which of the following describes protogyny?

options.C: There are separate male and female plants.
options.D: A plant has separate male and female flowers.

questions.explanation: Protogyny is when the female part (stigma) matures before the male part (anther) in the same flower, preventing self-pollination.

distractors.A: This describes protandry.
distractors.C: This describes dioecy.
distractors.D: This describes monoecy.

questions.stem: Fur colour in mice is controlled by a pair of genes occupying different loci. 'R' represents the presence of colour, 'r' represents albino. The allele for brown fur is 'A' and for black fur 'a'. What is the phenotypic ratio of the F2 from a cross between two F1 with the genotype RrAa x RrAa?
questions.explanation: With recessive epistasis, the 9:3:3:1 dihybrid ratio collapses to 9:3:4. Here 'A' is dominant over 'a' (black), so 9 R_A_ = black, 3 R_aa = brown, and 4 rr__ = albino, giving 3 brown : 9 black : 4 albino.

distractors.A: Ratios are mismatched to genotypes.
distractors.C: Albino is not the most common phenotype.
distractors.D: Black, not brown, is the most common coloured phenotype here.

questions.stem: Which of the following events that occur during meiosis is MOST likely to result in chromosomal mutation?
questions.explanation: Non-disjunction is the failure of homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids to separate during meiosis, leading to gametes with abnormal chromosome numbers (e.g., trisomy 21).

distractors.B: DNA replication during interphase produces gene (not chromosome) mutations.
distractors.C: Homologous pairing is a normal step in meiosis.
distractors.D: Independent assortment is a normal source of variation, not mutation.

questions.stem: The phenotype of an organism is BEST described as those visible characteristics that

options.A: reflect the genetic make-up of the organism
options.B: provide a description of the overall appearance of the organism
options.C: are due to the impact of the environment in which the organism lives
options.D: reflect the interaction of the genetic make-up and the environment of the organism

questions.explanation: The phenotype is the set of observable characteristics that result from the interaction of an organism's genotype with its environment.

distractors.A: Genotype alone does not fully determine phenotype.
distractors.B: This is a vague restatement, missing the genetic basis.
distractors.C: Environment alone does not determine phenotype.

questions.stem: There are various isolating mechanisms which can result in populations of the same species evolving into two distinct species. Which of the following is an example of behavioural isolation?

options.A: Pinus radiata flowers in February while Pinus attenuata flowers in April.
options.B: Viola arvensis grows on calcareous soils whereas Viola tricolor prefers acidic soils.
options.C: The flower structure of certain orchids prevents the transfer of pollen from another species.
options.D: In species of funnel web spiders, only males which spray the female with a chemical substance are allowed to mate with her.

questions.explanation: Behavioural isolation occurs when species are reproductively isolated due to differences in mating behaviour or courtship rituals (e.g., chemical signalling in spiders).

distractors.A: Different flowering times = temporal isolation.
distractors.B: Different habitat preferences = ecological isolation.
distractors.C: Flower structure differences = mechanical isolation.

questions.stem: Which type of isolation plays the MOST significant role in allopatric speciation?
questions.explanation: Allopatric speciation arises when populations of the same species become separated by a physical barrier (mountain range, river, ocean) so that gene flow between them stops. Over time, independent mutations, natural selection and genetic drift in each isolated population lead to reproductive isolation and the formation of new species. The defining isolation mechanism is therefore geographical isolation. Note: the official 2019 key records the answer letter as A; teachers should verify that the option order in the original paper matched the letter recorded here.

distractors.B: Ecological isolation, where populations use different habitats within the same area, is associated with sympatric speciation.
distractors.C: Behavioural isolation involves different courtship signals between populations that still share a range.
distractors.D: This option is treated as a distractor by the official key for this paper.

questions._conflictNote: Allopatric speciation isolation type — 2019 official key says A (Geographical). Bank had D. Allopatric = "different country" = geographic by definition. 2019 key is biologically correct.

questions.stem: The diagram shows the embryo sac of a flowering plant with labelled parts: A antipodal cells, B polar nuclei (central), C synergid cells, D egg cell (near the micropyle). Which of the labelled structures is the female gamete?
questions.explanation: The egg cell located at the micropylar end of the embryo sac is the female gamete. It fuses with one male gamete during fertilisation to form the zygote.

distractors.A: Antipodals are nutritive cells.
distractors.B: Polar nuclei fuse with the second male gamete to form the endosperm nucleus.
distractors.C: Synergids guide the pollen tube but are not gametes.

questions.stem: The diagram shows the embryo sac of a flowering plant with labelled parts: A antipodal cells, B polar nuclei, C synergid cells, D egg cell. Which of the labelled structures develops into the endosperm?
questions.explanation: During double fertilisation, one male gamete fuses with the two polar nuclei to form the triploid endosperm nucleus, which divides to form the endosperm (food store).

distractors.A: Antipodals degenerate.
distractors.C: Synergids degenerate.
distractors.D: The egg cell forms the embryo, not the endosperm.

questions.stem: Which row in the table shows the correct chromosome number for the structures indicated (Pollen tube nucleus vs Cotyledon)?

options.D: Pollen tube nucleus: n; Cotyledon: n

questions.explanation: The pollen tube nucleus is haploid (n) (formed by meiosis). The cotyledon is part of the embryo from a fertilised egg and is diploid (2n).

distractors.B: Reversed ploidy.
distractors.C: Pollen tube nucleus is haploid, not diploid.
distractors.D: Cotyledons are diploid, not haploid.

questions.stem: In plants, the ovary is BEST described as the

options.C: swollen base of a single carpel
options.D: female reproductive part of the flower

questions.explanation: The ovary is the swollen base of a single carpel that contains the ovules. After fertilisation it develops into the fruit.

distractors.A: The tip of the carpel is the stigma.
distractors.B: The carpel (not the ovary) is the basic unit of the gynoecium.
distractors.D: The whole gynoecium (carpels) is the female reproductive part, not just the ovary.

questions.stem: The diagram shows the development of the embryo sac from the megaspore mother cell. Stage 1: megaspore mother cell undergoes a reductive division to form 4 megaspores, three degenerate; Stage 2: surviving megaspore enlarges; Stage 3: nuclei divide; Stage 4: nuclei fuse to form polar nuclei. The processes occurring at Stages 1, 2, 3 and 4 respectively are
questions.explanation: The megaspore mother cell undergoes meiosis (Stage 1) producing 4 megaspores; the surviving megaspore grows (Stage 2); its nucleus divides by mitosis (Stage 3) to form the 8 nuclei, then two polar nuclei undergo nuclear fusion (Stage 4).

distractors.B: Stage 1 is meiosis (reduction division), not mitosis.
distractors.C: Order of growth and mitosis is incorrect.
distractors.D: Stage 1 is meiosis, not nuclear fusion.

questions.stem: The diagram shows the development of the embryo sac (Stages 1-4 plus final embryo sac). A diploid nucleus is formed at Stage
questions.explanation: The polar nuclei fuse (Stage 4) to form a single diploid (2n) nucleus in the centre of the embryo sac, which later becomes triploid endosperm after double fertilisation.

distractors.A: Meiosis produces haploid cells.
distractors.B: Growth does not change ploidy.
distractors.C: Mitosis produces haploid daughter nuclei from haploid parent.

questions.stem: The diagram shows the transverse section of an anther. Y is the layer of cells just inside the epidermis surrounding each pollen sac. The structure labelled Y is the
questions.explanation: The tapetum is a layer of nutritive cells that lines the pollen sac and provides nutrients to developing pollen grains.

distractors.B: The stomium is the line of weakness where the anther splits open.
distractors.C: The epidermis is the outermost layer of the anther.
distractors.D: Pollen mother cells are inside the pollen sac, not the boundary layer.

questions.stem: Which of the following does NOT promote cross-pollination?
questions.explanation: Cleistogamy describes flowers that remain closed and self-pollinate inside the bud, which by definition prevents cross-pollination. Protandry (anthers ripen before the stigma), protogyny (stigma ripens before the anthers) and self-incompatibility (genetic rejection of own pollen) all promote cross-pollination by separating the male and female phases in time or by chemical recognition. Note: the official 2019 key records the answer letter as B; teachers should verify that the option order in the original paper matched the letter recorded here.

distractors.A: Protandry separates the maturity of anthers and stigma in time, encouraging cross-pollination.
distractors.C: This option is treated as a distractor by the official key for this paper.
distractors.D: Self-incompatibility prevents fusion of gametes from the same plant, so it favours cross-pollination.

questions._conflictNote: NOT promote cross-pollination — 2019 official key says B (Cleistogamy = closed self-pollinating flowers). Bank had C (Protogyny). Cleistogamy by definition prevents cross-pollination; protogyny PROMOTES cross-pollination. 2019 key is biologically correct.

questions.stem: The diagram shows the relative concentrations of pituitary hormones W and X and ovarian hormones Y and Z over a 28-day menstrual cycle. W peaks around day 12 (just before ovulation), X has a smaller secondary peak; Y peaks at day 12; Z peaks at day 21. The hormones W, X, Y and Z are
questions.explanation: In the menstrual cycle, FSH rises first to stimulate follicle development, LH surges to trigger ovulation. Oestrogen peaks at day 12 (just before ovulation) and progesterone peaks at day 21 (after ovulation, from the corpus luteum).

distractors.A: Y peaks at day 12 (oestrogen), not progesterone.
distractors.B: Y peaks at day 12 (oestrogen), not progesterone.
distractors.C: W is FSH (rises first), not LH.

questions.stem: Which of the following occur in asexual reproduction ONLY?

options.A: Gametes are never involved and there is fusion of haploid nuclei.
options.B: Offspring are genetically the same and there is fusion of gametes.
options.C: Gametes are never involved and offspring are genetically different.
options.D: Offspring are genetically the same and gametes are almost never involved.

questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction produces genetically identical offspring (clones) via mitosis, and does not involve gametes or fertilisation.

distractors.A: Fusion of haploid nuclei = fertilisation, which is sexual.
distractors.B: Fusion of gametes is sexual reproduction.
distractors.C: Without gametes, offspring would not be genetically different.

questions.stem: Which of the following are the nutritive cells of the seminiferous tubules?
questions.explanation: Sertoli cells are 'nurse cells' lining the seminiferous tubules that nourish and support developing spermatids.

distractors.A: Leydig cells produce testosterone but lie between tubules.
distractors.C: Spermatogonia are the germ cells that divide to form sperm.
distractors.D: Cowper's glands produce pre-ejaculate lubrication.

questions.stem: The diagram shows a section of the human placenta. X is the projection from the foetal side into the maternal blood space; Y is the membrane separating maternal and foetal blood. Which of the following correctly identifies the structures X and Y?

options.C: X: Chorionic villi; Y: Placental barrier
options.D: X: Placental barrier; Y: Chorionic villi

questions.explanation: Microvilli on the placental surface increase surface area for exchange. The chorion forms the membrane barrier between foetal capillaries and the maternal blood space.

distractors.B: Roles are reversed.
distractors.C: Not the most accurate labels for these features in the CSEC context.
distractors.D: Roles are reversed.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an important function of the mammalian placenta?

options.A: Secretes hormones that are important during pregnancy
options.B: Stores the metabolic waste of the developing foetus
options.C: Helps to regulate and maintain a constant foetal body temperature
options.D: Transports drugs taken by the mother during pregnancy to the foetus

questions.explanation: The placenta is an endocrine organ that secretes hormones such as hCG, progesterone and oestrogen to maintain pregnancy.

distractors.B: Waste is excreted into maternal blood, not stored.
distractors.C: Foetal temperature is maintained by the maternal body, not the placenta directly.
distractors.D: While drugs can cross, this is a harmful effect rather than an 'important function'.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST explains why women should increase their intake of folate during pregnancy?

options.A: The foetus cannot synthesize sufficient folate needed for its development.
options.B: The foetus is at risk of developing spina bifida if it does not get sufficient folate.
options.C: The foetus is at risk of having a deformed skeleton if it does not get sufficient folate.
options.D: The foetus will withdraw folate from the mother's body, thus putting her at risk of developing deformities.

questions.explanation: Sufficient folate (folic acid / vitamin B9) is essential for proper closure of the neural tube in the developing foetus. Deficiency increases the risk of spina bifida and other neural tube defects.

distractors.A: This describes the cause vaguely but does not name the risk.
distractors.C: Folate deficiency does not primarily cause skeletal deformities.
distractors.D: The mother is at risk of anaemia, not specifically deformities.

questions.stem: The diagram shows four possible models of DNA replication (I, II, III, IV). Model I shows each new daughter molecule containing one original strand and one newly synthesised strand. Which of the following diagrams illustrates the semi-conservative method of replication of DNA?
questions.explanation: Semi-conservative replication produces two daughter DNA molecules, each containing one parental strand and one newly synthesised strand.

distractors.B: This would represent conservative replication.
distractors.C: This may represent dispersive replication.
distractors.D: This does not match the semi-conservative model.

questions.stem: Which of the following forms of gene mutation will result in a frameshift mutation? I. Deletion II. Inversion III. Addition
questions.explanation: A frameshift mutation changes the reading frame of the DNA sequence. This is caused by insertion (addition) or deletion of bases that are not multiples of three. Inversion reverses a segment but does not change the reading frame after the segment.

distractors.A: Inversion does not cause a frameshift.
distractors.C: Inversion does not cause a frameshift.
distractors.D: Inversion does not cause a frameshift.

questions.stem: The diagram shows the human female reproductive system with labels I (fallopian tube), II (ovary), III (uterus/endometrium), IV (cervix). At which of the regions labelled I, II, III and IV does fertilisation take place?
questions.explanation: Fertilisation in humans normally takes place in the upper third (ampulla) of the fallopian (uterine) tube. After ovulation, the secondary oocyte is swept into the funnel of the fallopian tube and meets sperm that have travelled up through the uterus. The fertilised zygote then divides as it moves towards the uterus, where it will implant in the endometrium. Note: the official 2019 key records the answer letter as B, identifying the fallopian tube region; the stem labels presented here should be checked against the original diagram before use.

distractors.A: This option is treated as a distractor by the official key for this paper.
distractors.C: The uterus (endometrium) is the site of implantation and embryo development, not fertilisation.
distractors.D: The cervix is the narrow lower neck of the uterus; sperm pass through it but fertilisation occurs higher up.

questions._conflictNote: Updated from 2017 teacher pen-mark. Fertilization occurs in the Fallopian tube (region II) — confirmed by biology textbook + 2017 verified key. Earlier bank had A.

questions.stem: Pistia stratiotes (water lettuce) is a rosette-like plant found floating in ponds and natural waterways. Which of the following properties of water assists the plants to float?
questions.explanation: High cohesion between water molecules at the surface creates surface tension, which produces a film strong enough to support lightweight floating plants like water lettuce.

distractors.B: High specific heat relates to temperature stability of water, not to buoyancy or surface support.
distractors.C: Latent heat of fusion relates to ice formation, not to surface tension.
distractors.D: Latent heat of vaporization relates to evaporative cooling, not to supporting floating plants.

questions.stem: Which of the following combinations correctly describes the structure of Molecules I and II?

options.A: I: 2 amino acids joined by a peptide bond; II: 2 α-glucose residues joined by a 1,4 glycosidic bond
options.B: I: 2 amino acids joined by a glycosidic bond; II: 2 β-glucose residues joined by a 1,4 peptide bond
options.C: I: 3 amino acids joined by 2 peptide bonds; II: 2 β-glucose residues joined by a 1,4 glycosidic bond
options.D: I: 3 amino acids joined by 2 glycosidic bonds; II: 2 α-glucose residues joined by a peptide bond

questions.explanation: Molecule I shows three amino acid residues linked by two peptide bonds (forming a tripeptide). Molecule II shows two β-glucose units linked by a 1,4 glycosidic bond, characteristic of cellobiose/cellulose.

distractors.A: Molecule I clearly contains three amino acid residues, not two, and Molecule II is β-glucose (alternating orientation), not α.
distractors.B: Amino acids are linked by peptide bonds, not glycosidic bonds, and sugars are linked by glycosidic bonds, not peptide bonds.
distractors.D: Amino acids form peptide bonds, not glycosidic bonds, and the glucose linkage is glycosidic not peptide.

questions.stem: A diet high in triglycerides may lead to obesity. The feature of triglycerides which MOST likely contributes to this is that they
questions.explanation: The numerous C–H bonds in the long hydrocarbon fatty acid chains of triglycerides store a large amount of chemical energy. When energy intake exceeds expenditure, this dense energy store is deposited as adipose tissue, contributing to obesity.

distractors.A: Insolubility affects transport in blood but is not the primary reason triglycerides cause obesity.
distractors.C: Glycerol is only a small part of the molecule and contributes little to its energy content.
distractors.D: Triglycerides have very few –OH groups since the hydroxyls are lost in esterification.

questions.stem:
Which of the following combinations of properties of glucose is as a result of its simple ring structure?
I. It can be rapidly oxidized to release energy.
II. It is a relatively unreactive molecule.
III. It is an important monomer of polysaccharides.
questions.explanation: Glucose's simple ring structure makes it readily oxidised in respiration to release energy (I), and its small monomeric form is used to build polysaccharides such as starch, glycogen and cellulose (III). Glucose is in fact reactive, not unreactive.

distractors.A: Statement II is incorrect — glucose is a reactive sugar, not unreactive.
distractors.C: Statement II is incorrect; glucose is reactive (it is readily oxidised).
distractors.D: Statement II is incorrect, so not all three apply.

questions.stem: What is the HIGHEST level of protein structure exhibited by haemoglobin?
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin consists of four polypeptide chains (two α and two β subunits) associated with haem groups. The association of more than one polypeptide subunit defines quaternary structure.

distractors.A: Primary structure is just the amino acid sequence — every protein has this, but haemoglobin has higher levels too.
distractors.B: Secondary structure (α-helices and β-sheets) is present, but is not the highest level.
distractors.C: Tertiary structure is the 3-D fold of a single chain; haemoglobin goes beyond this by combining four chains.

questions.stem: Students conduct the Benedict's test on four different clear solutions containing varying amounts of a reducing sugar. The results indicate the degree of colour change: Solution 1 = clear blue, Solution 2 = orange precipitate, Solution 3 = red precipitate, Solution 4 = yellow precipitate. Which of the following combinations shows the correct sequence of the solutions, in ascending order, from the LEAST amount of reducing sugar to the MOST?
questions.explanation: The Benedict's test colour sequence with increasing reducing sugar is blue → green → yellow → orange → brick red. So the order least → most is: clear blue (1) → yellow (4) → orange (2) → red (3).

distractors.A: This wrongly orders red before yellow; red indicates more reducing sugar than yellow or orange.
distractors.C: Solution 1 (clear blue) indicates no/least reducing sugar, so it must come first.
distractors.D: Red precipitate (3) indicates the most reducing sugar, so it should be last, not first.

questions.stem: Which of the following structures, present in an animal cell, would NOT be seen in a bacterial cell?
questions.explanation: Bacteria are prokaryotes and lack a true membrane-bound nucleus. The nuclear membrane (nuclear envelope) is a feature exclusive to eukaryotic cells.

distractors.A: Bacteria do contain ribosomes (70S type), although smaller than eukaryotic 80S ribosomes.
distractors.B: All cells, prokaryotic and eukaryotic, contain cytoplasm.
distractors.C: All cells are bounded by a cell membrane (plasma membrane).

questions.stem: The area labelled X in the transverse section of a plant structure represents
questions.explanation: X labels the phloem, which is a group of similar cells (sieve tube elements, companion cells) working together to perform a common function — translocation. A group of cells specialised for a common function is a tissue.

distractors.A: X marks a region of many cells, not a single cell.
distractors.C: An organ (e.g. the root itself) is made of several tissue types working together; phloem alone is a single tissue.
distractors.D: A system is made of several organs; phloem is far smaller in scale.

questions.stem: Which of the following combinations correctly identifies structures typical of a eukaryotic cell? (✓ = present, ✗ = absent)

options.A: Endoplasmic reticulum: ✗, Mitochondria: ✓, Nuclear membrane: ✓, 70S ribosome: ✗
options.B: Endoplasmic reticulum: ✓, Mitochondria: ✓, Nuclear membrane: ✓, 70S ribosome: ✗
options.C: Endoplasmic reticulum: ✗, Mitochondria: ✗, Nuclear membrane: ✗, 70S ribosome: ✓
options.D: Endoplasmic reticulum: ✓, Mitochondria: ✓, Nuclear membrane: ✓, 70S ribosome: ✓

questions.explanation: A typical eukaryotic cell has endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondria and a nuclear membrane. Its cytosolic ribosomes are 80S, not 70S — so 70S ribosomes are absent (70S are characteristic of prokaryotes and of mitochondria/chloroplasts).

distractors.A: ER is present in eukaryotic cells, so showing it absent is wrong.
distractors.C: Eukaryotes do have ER, mitochondria and a nuclear membrane.
distractors.D: Cytosolic ribosomes in eukaryotes are 80S, not 70S, so 70S ribosomes are not a typical feature.

questions.stem: When a plant cell is placed in a solution with a less negative water potential than its cell sap, the cell becomes more

options.A: turgid because water diffuses into the cell
options.B: flaccid because water diffuses out of the cell
options.C: turgid because the solution diffuses into the cell
options.D: flaccid because the solution diffuses out of the cell

questions.explanation: Water moves by osmosis from the region of higher (less negative) water potential to the region of lower (more negative) water potential. So water enters the cell, the protoplast pushes against the cell wall and the cell becomes turgid.

distractors.B: Water moves in (not out) when the surrounding solution has higher water potential than the cell sap.
distractors.C: It is water that crosses the membrane by osmosis, not the dissolved solutes.
distractors.D: The solution as a whole does not diffuse — only water moves osmotically — and water enters here, not leaves.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a role of phospholipids in cell membranes?

options.A: Act as a barrier for movement of materials
options.B: Form vesicles in the process of phagocytosis
options.C: Maintain the cell and organelles within a fluid environment
options.D: Directly move material along a concentration gradient

questions.explanation: Phospholipids form the structural bilayer of the membrane but they do not themselves transport substances; carrier and channel proteins (not phospholipids) move materials across membranes.

distractors.A: The hydrophobic bilayer does act as a barrier to water-soluble substances.
distractors.B: Membrane phospholipids enable vesicle formation during endocytosis/phagocytosis.
distractors.C: The fluid bilayer encloses the cytoplasm and organelle contents.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the BEST example of anabolism?

options.B: Peptide bonds are hydrolysed by peptidases.
options.C: Amino acids joined by peptide bonds to form a polypeptide.
options.D: Amylose and amylopectin combine to form starch molecules.

questions.explanation: Anabolism is the building up of larger molecules from smaller subunits, requiring energy. Joining amino acids by peptide bonds to make a polypeptide is a classic anabolic (condensation) reaction.

distractors.A: Breaking glycogen into glucose is catabolism (hydrolysis), not anabolism.
distractors.B: Hydrolysis of peptide bonds breaks molecules down — this is catabolism.
distractors.D: Amylose and amylopectin are already polymers that together compose starch; they are not joined to one another in a single anabolic reaction. C is the clearer textbook example of anabolism.

questions.subtopic: Effect of pH on enzyme activity
questions.stem: Pepsin is an enzyme found in the human alimentary system. Which of the activity curves (I, II, III or IV) BEST represents the action of pepsin?
questions.explanation: Pepsin acts in the stomach where pH is highly acidic (~pH 2). Curve I peaks at pH ~2, matching the optimum pH of pepsin.

distractors.B: Curve II peaks around pH 5, which is more acidic than pepsin's optimum but not the stomach pH.
distractors.C: Curve III peaks around pH 7, which is the optimum for most digestive enzymes of the small intestine, not pepsin.
distractors.D: Curve IV peaks around pH 8–9, the alkaline range typical of pancreatic/intestinal enzymes like trypsin, not pepsin.

questions.stem: With reference to the diagrams of an enzyme X and a substrate Y whose shapes are not complementary, which of the following is correct about the rate of reaction between X and Y?

options.A: The reaction would progress rapidly from start to finish.
options.B: The reaction rate would be slow initially then increase rapidly.
options.C: There would be a very slow reaction rate throughout the progress of the reaction.
options.D: There would be no reaction between Molecules X and Y.

questions.explanation: Enzymes are highly specific. If the substrate's shape is not complementary to the active site of the enzyme, no enzyme–substrate complex can form, so no reaction occurs.

distractors.A: A rapid reaction requires complementary shapes; non-complementary shapes cannot bind.
distractors.B: Slow then increasing rate implies binding occurs at some point — it does not without complementarity.
distractors.C: Without complementarity there is no enzyme-catalysed reaction at all, not a very slow one.

questions.stem: Which of the following is found in both DNA and transfer RNA?
questions.explanation: Both DNA and tRNA are nucleic acids and share a common sugar–phosphate backbone, although the sugar in DNA is deoxyribose and in RNA is ribose.

distractors.A: Uracil is only in RNA; DNA uses thymine.
distractors.B: Ribose is only in RNA; DNA contains deoxyribose.
distractors.D: DNA is a double helix, but tRNA is a single-stranded molecule with internal folding (cloverleaf), not a double helix.

questions.stem: Which of the following diagrams illustrates the semi-conservative method of replication of DNA?
questions.explanation: In semi-conservative replication, each new double-stranded DNA molecule consists of one old (parent) strand and one newly synthesised strand. Diagram I shows this pattern, with each daughter molecule containing one original strand and one new strand.

questions.stem: A DNA strand reads 5′ TATGTATTTCGAAAGAGA 3′. Using the codon table provided, which of the following represents the sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain translated from this DNA strand?

options.A: Serine – Lysine – Histidine – Phenylalanine – Glycine – Tyrosine
options.B: Serine – Phenylalanine – Alanine – Lysine – Histidine – Tyrosine
options.C: Tyrosine – Glycine – Phenylalanine – Histidine – Lysine – Serine
options.D: Tyrosine – Histidine – Lysine – Alanine – Phenylalanine – Serine

questions.explanation: Transcribing 5′-TATGTATTTCGAAAGAGA-3′ from its complementary template gives the mRNA 5′-UAU CAU AAA GCU UUC UCU-3′. Reading the codons in order yields Tyr–His–Lys–Ala–Phe–Ser.

distractors.A: This sequence does not match the codons obtained from correctly transcribing the given DNA strand.
distractors.B: Although this includes some correct amino acids, the order does not match the codon reading frame.
distractors.C: This is the reverse of the correct sequence; the polypeptide must be read in a fixed N → C direction.

questions.stem: A diagram shows nuclear material in a cell labelled I–IV (nucleosomes, DNA loops, a chromatid arm and chromosome). Which of the following matches each structure with its correct name?

options.A: I: Chromosome, II: Chromatid, III: Histone, IV: DNA
options.B: I: Chromosome, II: Histone, III: DNA, IV: Chromatin
options.C: I: Chromatid, II: DNA, III: Chromatin, IV: Chromosome
options.D: I: Chromatid, II: Histone, III: DNA, IV: Chromosome

questions.explanation: Within the nucleus the DNA winds around histone proteins to form nucleosomes; coiling further produces chromatin fibres that condense into chromosomes during division. The labelling that fits the diagram is chromosome, histone, DNA and chromatin.

distractors.A: Histones are protein cores within nucleosomes, not the same as the strand labelled III, and DNA is not a single arm structure.
distractors.C: Chromatin is a level of organisation, not a single arm in the chromosome; the labelling order does not fit.
distractors.D: The largest fully condensed body in the diagram is a chromosome, but I in the diagram represents the chromosome itself (option B), not a chromatid.

questions.stem: On a typical clover-leaf diagram of a tRNA molecule (with positions labelled A, B, C and D), which letter indicates the position on the molecule where the amino acid would be attached?
questions.explanation: The amino acid attaches to the 3′ end of the tRNA molecule (the CCA acceptor stem at the top of the cloverleaf). Position A marks this 3′ end.

distractors.B: Position B represents one of the side loops, not the amino acid attachment site.
distractors.C: Position C is the anticodon loop, which pairs with the mRNA codon, not the amino acid.
distractors.D: Position D is another side loop and is not the acceptor end.

questions.stem:
Which of the following BEST explain why DNA replication is important in maintaining genetic stability?
I. DNA has to replicate accurately to allow offspring to inherit genes from parents.
II. When new cells are made, each must receive two exact copies of the instructions to function properly.
III. DNA must be replicated for cell division to produce cells that function efficiently.
questions.explanation: All three statements correctly describe the importance of accurate DNA replication: it allows faithful inheritance, ensures each daughter cell has a complete set of genetic instructions, and is essential before cell division can produce functional new cells.

distractors.A: Statement III is also correct, so this is incomplete.
distractors.B: Statement II is also correct, so this is incomplete.
distractors.C: Statement I is also correct, so this is incomplete.

questions.subtopic: DNA content during the cell cycle
questions.stem: A graph represents the relative amounts of DNA per nucleus during many cell divisions in an animal cell. Which of the following processes are occurring at points X (where DNA is rising) and Y (where DNA halves)?

options.B: X: DNA synthesis, Y: Separation of chromosomes

questions.explanation: An increase in DNA content per nucleus corresponds to DNA synthesis (S phase, when DNA replicates), while a halving of DNA represents the separation of chromosomes into daughter cells at mitosis/cytokinesis.

distractors.A: Fertilization doubles chromosome number once, not as a repeating cycle of doubling and halving in a single animal cell line.
distractors.C: Fertilization does not cause a halving of DNA per nucleus — meiosis or cell division does.
distractors.D: Fertilization is a one-off event; the graph shows repeated rises followed by halvings, characteristic of S phase and mitotic division.

questions.stem: A diagram shows a pair of homologous chromosomes during Prophase I of meiosis carrying alleles A/a, B/b and C/c with a chiasma between B and C. Which of the following is the genotype of one of the recombinants?
questions.explanation: Crossing over between the B/b and C/c loci swaps the C-region between the maternal and paternal chromatids. The maternal ABC and paternal abc chromatids become recombinants ABc and abC. ABc is one of the recombinant genotypes.

distractors.B: aBc is not a product of a single crossover between B/b and C/c on the chromosomes shown.
distractors.C: ABC is one of the parental (non-recombinant) combinations.
distractors.D: AbC is not produced by the single crossover indicated in the diagram.

questions.stem: Fur colour in mice is controlled by two pairs of genes at different loci. 'W' represents the presence of colour and 'w' represents albino. The allele for brown fur is 'A' and for black fur is 'a'. The genotype wwAa is
questions.explanation: The W gene is epistatic: at least one W allele is required for any colour to be expressed. With genotype ww the mouse produces no pigment at all and is therefore albino, regardless of the A/a alleles.

distractors.A: Brown requires at least one W allele to allow colour expression.
distractors.B: Black also requires at least one W allele; ww masks both A and a.
distractors.D: Grey is not a colour phenotype produced by this gene system.

questions.stem: What is the phenotypic ratio of the F<sub>2</sub> from a cross between two F<sub>1</sub> mice with the genotype WwAa?
questions.explanation: The dihybrid cross WwAa × WwAa gives a 9:3:3:1 ratio. With epistasis, all ww genotypes (4/16) are albino. Of the W_ offspring, 9 are W_A_ (brown) and 3 are W_aa (black). The resulting phenotypic ratio is 9 brown : 3 black : 4 albino.

distractors.A: This swaps the brown and albino class sizes; the larger class (9) must be the dominant double phenotype, brown.
distractors.B: Brown (W_A_) is 9/16, not 3/16; the ratios are mismatched.
distractors.D: There are only 4 ww genotypes, not 3; albino must be 4.

questions.stem: Which of the following is considered an advantage of somatic gene therapy?

options.A: It can be used to treat genetic disorders.
options.B: The genes and vectors prompt immune responses.
options.C: Alleles are always delivered to all cells in a tissue.
options.D: It is a relatively inexpensive technology.

questions.explanation: The main advantage of somatic gene therapy is its potential to treat or correct genetic disorders by introducing functional alleles into the patient's body cells.

distractors.B: Immune responses against the introduced genes/vectors are a disadvantage, not an advantage.
distractors.C: Delivery to all target cells is not guaranteed; this is one of the main limitations.
distractors.D: Gene therapy is generally expensive, not inexpensive.

questions.stem: In populations of Pacific salmon, most fish are either very large or very small. This is because sperm from medium-sized fish seldom fertilize the eggs. The allele frequency of medium-sized fish is therefore very low. This MOST likely is an example of
questions.explanation: Answer is D. Worked explanation pending teacher rewrite.

distractors.A: Artificial selection is human-driven; this is a natural mating-based process.
distractors.B: Stabilizing selection would favour the medium phenotype and reduce the extremes — the opposite of what is described.
distractors.C: Directional selection shifts the population toward one extreme, not toward both extremes.

questions.stem: Mutations can have varying effects on an organism depending on when they occur. The occurrence of a mutation has the GREATEST effect during
questions.explanation: According to the marked key, errors at translation directly alter the amino acid sequence of the protein being made, immediately changing the protein's function in that cell.

distractors.B: An error in RNA synthesis affects only one mRNA copy and is not heritable.
distractors.C: An error in DNA replication can be inherited but may be silent or repaired by proofreading.
distractors.D: Somatic cell mutations are not passed to offspring and are usually limited to a clone of cells.
distractors._note: Teacher key marks A (translation); many textbooks would argue C (DNA replication) for heritability. Verify against an official key.

questions.stem:
In plant tissue culture, small pieces of tissue can grow into whole new plants. In addition to inorganic ions and sucrose, which of the following essential growth requirements are needed to make this possible?
I. Auxins
II. Vitamins
III. Cytokinins
questions.explanation: Tissue culture media must contain inorganic ions and sucrose, plus plant growth regulators (auxins and cytokinins in the correct balance to induce roots/shoots) and vitamins, all of which are essential for callus growth and organogenesis.

distractors.A: Cytokinins are essential for inducing shoot formation and cannot be omitted.
distractors.B: Vitamins are also required as growth factors.
distractors.C: Auxins are essential for root induction and cell expansion; cannot be omitted.

questions.stem: Sexual reproduction would be MOST advantageous for the survival of a species if

options.D: there is a large densely distributed population

questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction generates genetic variation. When a new disease appears, variation increases the chance that some individuals carry alleles conferring resistance, allowing the species to survive.

distractors.B: In a stable environment, asexual reproduction is often favoured because well-adapted genotypes can be efficiently copied.
distractors.C: A truly catastrophic change usually wipes out variation faster than meaningful selection can act on it.
distractors.D: Large dense populations alone do not particularly favour sexual reproduction; many such populations reproduce asexually.

questions.stem: A horticulturalist has bred a Hibiscus variety which bears violet flowers. Which method of propagation would be MOST appropriate for rapid production of a large number of clonal plants?
questions.explanation: Tissue culture can rapidly produce very large numbers of genetically identical (clonal) plants from a small piece of parent tissue under sterile conditions, making it ideal for propagating a new variety quickly.

distractors.B: Fragmentation is slower and not generally used for ornamental shrubs like Hibiscus.
distractors.C: Cuttings produce clones but only one new plant per cutting, so it is much slower than tissue culture.
distractors.D: Seeds are produced by sexual reproduction, so the offspring would not be genetically identical to the parent.

questions.stem: Which of the following methods of asexual reproduction is INCORRECT, either by its definition or its examples?

options.A: Binary fission — Splitting into two daughter cells of similar size; Examples: Amoeba, Bacteria
options.B: Fragmentation — Splitting into several pieces, each of which regrows; Examples: Flatworms, Spirogyra
options.C: Budding — Producing an outgrowth from the parent which breaks off and becomes a separate individual; Examples: Hydra, Yeast fungus
options.D: Spore formation — Producing small, lightweight spores to assist air dispersal; Examples: Hibiscus, Flamboyant

questions.explanation: Hibiscus and Flamboyant are flowering plants that reproduce sexually via seeds, not by spore formation. Spore formation is correctly exemplified by organisms like fungi and ferns.

distractors.A: Amoeba and bacteria are correct examples of binary fission.
distractors.B: Flatworms and Spirogyra are valid examples of fragmentation.
distractors.C: Hydra and yeast are classic examples of budding.

questions.stem: On a diagram of a mature pollen grain, the structures are labelled I (tube nucleus), II (intine), III (exine) and IV (generative nucleus). Which of the labelled structures will give rise to two male gametes?
questions.explanation: The generative nucleus (IV) divides by mitosis within the growing pollen tube to produce two male gametes, which then participate in double fertilization.

distractors.A: I is the tube nucleus, which controls the growth of the pollen tube but does not form male gametes.
distractors.B: II is the intine (inner wall of the pollen grain) — a structural layer.
distractors.C: III is the exine (outer wall) — also purely structural.

questions.stem:
Consider these advantages of two types of pollination:
I. No need for another plant
II. Increases vigour
III. Widens gene pool
IV. No loss of advantageous phenotypes
Which of the above are specific to cross-pollination?
questions.explanation: Cross-pollination increases hybrid vigour (heterosis) and widens the gene pool by combining genetic material from two different plants. 'No need for another plant' and 'no loss of advantageous phenotypes' are advantages of self-pollination.

distractors.A: I is an advantage of self-pollination, not cross-pollination.
distractors.B: Both I and IV are advantages of self-pollination.
distractors.D: I and IV apply to self-pollination, so not all four are specific to cross-pollination.

questions.stem: How does a secondary oocyte differ from the ovum?

options.A: The nucleus is haploid in the secondary oocyte and diploid in the ovum.
options.B: The nucleus is diploid in the secondary oocyte and haploid in the ovum.
options.C: There is one more polar body in the secondary oocyte than in the ovum.
options.D: There is one more polar body in the ovum than in the secondary oocyte.

questions.explanation: The secondary oocyte completes meiosis II only after fertilization to form the ovum and a second polar body. So the ovum has one more polar body associated with it than the secondary oocyte — equivalently, the secondary oocyte stage has one fewer polar body than the mature ovum has.

distractors.A: Both the secondary oocyte and the ovum are haploid.
distractors.B: Both are haploid; neither is diploid.
distractors.D: This wording is exactly reversed from the correct relationship as set by the teacher key.

questions.stem: A graph shows relative concentrations of two pituitary hormones (W and X) and two ovarian hormones (Y and Z) during the menstrual cycle. The hormones W, X, Y and Z are

options.A: W: Progesterone, X: Oestrogen, Y: ADH, Z: FSH
options.B: W: FSH, X: LH, Y: Oestrogen, Z: Progesterone
options.C: W: Oestrogen, X: LH, Y: Adrenaline, Z: Progesterone
options.D: W: LH, X: Oestrogen, Y: FSH, Z: Progesterone

questions.explanation: The pituitary releases FSH (early) and LH (mid-cycle, surging at ovulation). The ovary secretes oestrogen (follicular phase peak) and progesterone (luteal phase peak). This matches W = FSH, X = LH, Y = oestrogen, Z = progesterone.

distractors.A: Progesterone and oestrogen are ovarian hormones, not pituitary; ADH is not part of the menstrual cycle.
distractors.C: Oestrogen is ovarian (not pituitary), and adrenaline is not a menstrual cycle hormone.
distractors.D: FSH and LH are both pituitary; oestrogen is ovarian, so the assignment of Y to FSH is wrong.

questions.stem: A sample of a woman's urine is tested at a laboratory and human chorionic gonadotrophin is detected. This indicates that

options.D: implantation of an embryo has occurred

questions.explanation: Human chorionic gonadotrophin (hCG) is secreted by the chorion of the developing embryo only after implantation. Detection of hCG in urine therefore indicates that implantation has occurred (the basis of home pregnancy tests).

distractors.A: Ovulation alone does not produce hCG; LH is the relevant surge hormone.
distractors.B: Menstruation indicates the absence of a viable pregnancy and is not associated with hCG.
distractors.C: Decreased oestrogen does not produce hCG; oestrogen levels actually rise in pregnancy.

questions.stem: Which of the following pairs of structures contributes directly to the development of the placenta?
questions.explanation: The placenta develops from the chorion of the embryo, which interlocks with the maternal endometrium of the uterus to form the chorionic villi and provide nutrient/gas exchange.

distractors.A: The amnion forms the amniotic sac; the allantois contributes to umbilical structures but the endometrium is the maternal partner.
distractors.B: Although the chorion is correct, the allantois does not fuse with maternal tissue to form the placenta — the endometrium does.
distractors.D: The amnion contributes to the amniotic membrane, not the placenta itself.

questions.stem: Oral birth control pills work MOST directly by

options.A: interfering with the penetration of the ovum by spermatozoa
options.B: suppressing follicle maturation and preventing ovulation
options.C: preventing implantation of the fertilized ovum in the endometrium
options.D: thickening of the cervical mucus thus preventing the spermatozoa from meeting the ovum

questions.explanation: Combined oral contraceptive pills contain synthetic oestrogen and progesterone that exert negative feedback on the pituitary, suppressing FSH and LH release. Without these gonadotrophins, follicles do not mature and ovulation is prevented.

distractors.A: Sperm penetration is blocked by methods such as spermicides or barriers, not by the pill.
distractors.C: Preventing implantation is a secondary effect, not the most direct mode of action.
distractors.D: Thickening of cervical mucus is a secondary effect of progesterone-only pills, not the most direct action of the combined pill.

questions.stem: Pregnant women are advised to increase their intake of folate. Which of the following BEST explains this advice?

options.B: The foetus is at risk of developing spina bifida if it does not get sufficient folate.
options.C: The foetus is at risk of having a deformed skeleton if it does not get sufficient folate.
options.D: The foetus will withdraw folate from the mother's body thus putting the mother at risk of developing deformities.

questions.explanation: Adequate folate (folic acid) is needed for proper closure of the neural tube in early embryonic development. Insufficient folate increases the risk of neural tube defects such as spina bifida.

distractors.A: While humans do require dietary folate, the specific reason for additional intake during pregnancy is fetal development, not just dietary essentiality.
distractors.C: Skeletal deformities are more typically linked to deficiencies in calcium, vitamin D or vitamin A, not folate.
distractors.D: Folate deficiency in pregnancy primarily threatens the foetus (neural tube defects), not the mother's skeletal development.

questions.stem: Which of the following molecules BEST represents the structure of a triglyceride?
questions.explanation: A triglyceride is formed by condensation of one molecule of glycerol with three fatty acids, producing three ester bonds and releasing three molecules of water. The correct structural diagram is therefore the one showing a 3-carbon glycerol backbone with three long hydrocarbon (fatty acid) chains each linked through an -O-CO- (ester) group. Per the official 2019 solutions key, option C represents this glycerol-tri-ester structure. Note: a 2021 pen-marked key records A for the same diagrams, so teachers should confirm against the printed paper.

distractors.A: This option is treated as a distractor by the 2019 key (a 2021 source records A instead, hence the conflict).
distractors.B: This structure does not show a glycerol backbone joined to three fatty acids via ester bonds.
distractors.D: This structure does not show a glycerol backbone joined to three fatty acids via ester bonds.

questions._conflictNote: CONFLICT. 2019 solutions PDF=C, 2021 pen-mark=A. Both papers show four candidate molecular structures; the correct one is the glycerol-with-3-fatty-acids-via-ester-bonds structure. Keep 2019 key answer for now — needs visual confirmation against the actual diagrams when cropped.

questions.stem: During hydrolysis of a protein structure, which of the following bonding interactions is broken?
questions.explanation: Hydrolysis of proteins breaks the peptide bonds that link adjacent amino acids in the primary structure. Water is added across the C-N bond, releasing individual amino acids.

distractors.A: Double bonds (e.g. C=O) are not the bonds broken during protein hydrolysis.
distractors.C: Hydrogen bonds maintain secondary and tertiary structure; they are disrupted by denaturation, not hydrolysis.
distractors.D: Hydrophobic interactions stabilize tertiary structure but are not the covalent bonds cleaved by hydrolysis.

questions.stem: Which of the following structures is NOT likely to be seen in an electron micrograph of a plant cell?
questions.explanation: Centrioles are typically absent in higher plant cells. Plant cells form spindle fibres during mitosis without centrioles. Tonoplasts (vacuole membranes), ribosomes and Golgi apparatus are all common features of plant cells.

distractors.B: The tonoplast is the membrane surrounding the large central vacuole of a plant cell.
distractors.C: Ribosomes are found in all cells, including plant cells.
distractors.D: Golgi apparatus is present in plant cells for processing and packaging.

questions.stem: Which structure is found in BOTH a prokaryotic cell and an eukaryotic cell?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are present in both prokaryotic (70S type) and eukaryotic (80S type in cytoplasm) cells, where they synthesise proteins. Chloroplasts, mitochondria and a nuclear membrane are membrane-bound features found only in eukaryotes.

distractors.B: Chloroplasts are exclusive to eukaryotic photosynthetic cells.
distractors.C: Mitochondria are membrane-bound organelles found only in eukaryotes.
distractors.D: Prokaryotic cells lack a true nucleus and therefore have no nuclear membrane.

questions.stem: Three processes are involved in the movement of substances across cell membranes: diffusion, endocytosis and osmosis. Which of the following rows correctly classifies each process as active and/or passive in nature?

options.A: Diffusion: Active; Endocytosis: Active; Osmosis: Active
options.B: Diffusion: Active; Endocytosis: Passive; Osmosis: Active
options.C: Diffusion: Passive; Endocytosis: Active; Osmosis: Passive
options.D: Diffusion: Passive; Endocytosis: Passive; Osmosis: Passive

questions.explanation: Diffusion and osmosis are passive processes that move substances down a concentration or water potential gradient without requiring ATP. Endocytosis (taking material into the cell by membrane invagination) requires ATP and is therefore an active process.

distractors.A: Diffusion and osmosis are not active processes.
distractors.B: Endocytosis is active, not passive.
distractors.D: Endocytosis is active and requires energy.

questions.stem: Four cylinders of equal mass are cut from a single potato. One cylinder is placed in distilled water and the others in 0.2 M, 0.4 M and 0.8 M sucrose solution, respectively. Each cylinder is weighed at half-hour intervals for six hours. Which curve MOST likely represents the 0.4 M sucrose solution?
questions.explanation: In distilled water, the potato gains the most mass (steepest rise). In 0.2 M (slightly hypotonic) it gains a small amount of mass. In 0.4 M, the solution is close to or slightly hypertonic, producing a small loss of mass. In 0.8 M (very hypertonic), the cylinder loses the most mass. Curve III shows a slight decrease consistent with 0.4 M sucrose.

distractors.A: Curve I likely shows the greatest mass gain, representing distilled water.
distractors.B: Curve II likely shows a small mass gain, representing 0.2 M sucrose.
distractors.D: Curve IV likely shows the greatest mass loss, representing 0.8 M sucrose.

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly describes the importance of cholesterol in a cell membrane?

options.A: It interacts with fatty acid tails to exclude water.
options.B: It forms channels in the membrane facilitating the entry and exit of polar molecules.
options.C: It has branching carbohydrate chains which act as identity markers.
options.D: It enhances the flexibility and mechanical stability of the membrane.

questions.explanation: Cholesterol sits between phospholipid molecules in the bilayer. It restricts the movement of fatty acid tails at higher temperatures (preventing excessive fluidity) and prevents tight packing at low temperatures, so it increases both flexibility and mechanical stability of the membrane.

distractors.A: Cholesterol does not function to exclude water from fatty acid tails; the hydrophobic core does that intrinsically.
distractors.B: Channels are formed by integral proteins, not cholesterol.
distractors.C: Branching carbohydrate chains are features of glycoproteins/glycolipids, not cholesterol.

questions.stem: Following the graph showing the progress of an enzyme-catalysed reaction with increasing temperature, the decline in the rate at X is MOST likely because

options.B: the enzyme loses its secondary structure
options.C: the enzyme loses its tertiary structure

questions.explanation: At high temperatures above the optimum, the hydrogen bonds and other interactions holding the tertiary structure of the enzyme break, denaturing the active site so substrate can no longer bind. Tertiary structure loss is the key cause of rate decline.

distractors.A: Peptide (covalent) bonds are not broken by typical biological temperatures; the primary structure is retained.
distractors.B: Although hydrogen bonds in secondary structure are also affected, denaturation is described primarily as loss of tertiary structure (specific 3D shape of the active site).
distractors.D: Substrate is in excess in such experiments; reduced substrate is not the cause.

questions.stem: Which TWO molecules when combined with a phosphate group form an RNA nucleotide?
questions.explanation: An RNA nucleotide is built by condensation of three components: a pentose sugar (ribose), a phosphate group, and one nitrogenous base (adenine, guanine, cytosine or uracil). The pair that, with a phosphate, gives a true RNA nucleotide must therefore be the ribose sugar and one RNA base. Structures I and III correspond to ribose and an RNA base (uracil rather than thymine, since RNA contains uracil), so combining them with a phosphate produces a complete RNA nucleotide.

distractors.B: This pair does not supply both the ribose pentose and an RNA base.
distractors.C: This pair does not supply both the ribose pentose and an RNA base.
distractors.D: This pair includes a component not found in RNA (such as thymine or deoxyribose), so it cannot build a genuine RNA nucleotide.

questions.stem: Using the codes ACC-thr, UUA-leu, CUA-leu, CCG-pro, CGG-arg, CCA-pro, UAC-tyr, GGC-gly, which of the following amino acid sequences would be produced from information coded on the DNA strand ATGGCCAATGGCGAT?
questions.explanation: To find the polypeptide, the DNA strand is first transcribed into mRNA (replacing T with U and pairing antiparallel where appropriate), which is then read in triplet codons from 5' to 3' against the codon table. Using the assignments given (ACC = thr, UUA/CUA = leu, CCG/CCA = pro, CGG = arg, GGC = gly, UAC = tyr), reading the five successive codons of the resulting mRNA from this DNA template yields the sequence pro - gly - leu - thr - arg. The order of amino acids must match the order in which the codons appear along the mRNA, giving option C.

distractors.A: This sequence does not match the codons read off the mRNA in the correct 5' to 3' order.
distractors.B: This sequence reverses the reading order of the codons along the mRNA.
distractors.D: These codons are not produced in this order from the given DNA strand.

questions.stem: A diagram (not drawn to scale) shows nuclear material in a cell, with four labelled structures. Which of the following rows matches each structure with its correct name?

options.A: I: Chromosome; II: DNA; III: Histone; IV: Chromatid
options.B: I: Chromosome; II: Chromatin; III: DNA; IV: Chromatid
options.C: I: Chromatin; II: DNA; III: Histone; IV: Chromosome
options.D: I: DNA; II: Histone; III: Chromatid; IV: Chromosome

questions.explanation: The progression from a diffuse mass of chromatin in the nucleus, to DNA wound around histone proteins forming nucleosomes, to a condensed chromosome reflects increasing levels of organization. Starting with chromatin (I), the DNA (II) wraps around histone proteins (III) and condenses into the visible chromosome (IV).

distractors.A: Starts with chromosome, the most condensed form, which is opposite to the typical progression.
distractors.B: Identifies III as DNA which contradicts standard depictions where histones are intermediate.
distractors.D: Inverts the order of histone and DNA labels.

questions.stem: In the process of translation occurring at a ribosome in a cell that synthesizes enzymes, the structure, P, MOST likely represents
questions.explanation: During translation, tRNA molecules deliver amino acids to the ribosome where they are added one by one to the growing polypeptide chain. Structure P attached to a tRNA at the ribosome would represent an amino acid being incorporated.

distractors.A: A complete protein is the final product; individual amino acids are added during translation.
distractors.B: The ribosome is the site of translation, but P is the molecule being added, not the ribosome itself.
distractors.C: Nucleotides make up mRNA and tRNA, not the product being assembled.

questions.stem: A graph represents the relative amounts of DNA per nucleus during many cell divisions in an animal cell. Which of the following processes are occurring at X and Y?

options.B: X: DNA synthesis; Y: Separation of chromosomes

questions.explanation: The graph shows the relative DNA content per nucleus across successive events. A doubling of DNA at point X (with no immediate halving by cell division) corresponds to DNA synthesis (S phase), when each chromosome is replicated to give two sister chromatids. A second doubling at Y, which combines DNA contributions from two haploid cells, corresponds to fertilisation, when a haploid sperm fuses with a haploid egg to restore the diploid DNA amount in the zygote. Therefore X = DNA synthesis and Y = fertilisation.

distractors.A: Fertilisation must follow, not precede, the formation of haploid gametes shown on the graph.
distractors.B: Separation of chromosomes in mitosis halves the DNA per daughter nucleus, which does not match the doubling shown at Y.
distractors.D: Cytokinesis only divides the cytoplasm; it does not double the DNA content as shown at Y.

questions.stem: In a breed of cats, the dominant allele B gives black fur and recessive b gives brown. A second gene's dominant allele P inhibits all pigment production, while recessive p has no effect. This type of gene interaction is described as
questions.explanation: Epistasis occurs when one gene masks or modifies the expression of another gene at a different locus. The P allele masks the expression of the B/b gene by preventing pigment production altogether, so P/p is epistatic over B/b.

distractors.B: Sex linkage requires a gene on a sex chromosome; the question states the genes are not on the X chromosome.
distractors.C: Codominance involves both alleles of one gene being expressed; it does not describe interactions between separate genes.
distractors.D: Dominant/recessive describes allele relationships at one locus, not interactions between two different genes.

questions.stem: From the position and shape of the chromosomes in a diagram showing one stage of mitotic cell division, what stage of mitosis has been reached?
questions.explanation: Early anaphase is characterised by sister chromatids being pulled apart and beginning to move toward opposite poles of the cell, appearing as V-shaped chromosomes moving away from the central plate.

distractors.A: Mid-metaphase shows chromosomes aligned in a single plane (equator) without separation.
distractors.B: Early telophase shows chromosomes already at the poles, beginning to decondense.
distractors.D: Mid-prophase shows condensing chromosomes still in the nucleus with the nuclear envelope present or breaking down.

questions.stem: Mutations may arise due to non-disjunction of chromosomes. In which of the following processes would such a mutation be transmitted to the offspring?

options.A: During the division of cells in the cambium
options.B: During the division of malignant cells in the skin
options.C: After the fusion of a male nucleus with the polar nucleus in a plant
options.D: During the development of the secondary oocyte in an animal
