questions.explanation: Mutations are only inherited by offspring when they occur in gamete-producing cells (germline). A non-disjunction event during meiosis in a developing secondary oocyte will produce a gamete with abnormal chromosome number, which when fertilized will pass the mutation to the offspring.

distractors.A: Cambium cells are somatic plant cells; mutations there are not transmitted to offspring through sexual reproduction.
distractors.B: Malignant skin cells are somatic; their mutations cannot be passed to offspring.
distractors.C: Fusion with the polar nucleus produces endosperm tissue, which nourishes the embryo but is not part of the next generation's genome.

questions.stem: A possible benefit of producing transgenic plants is
questions.explanation: Transgenic plants can be engineered to have higher nutritional content (e.g. Golden Rice with beta-carotene), improving human nutrition. They may also reduce pesticide use, increase yields and tolerate stresses.

distractors.A: Determination of the human genome is not related to transgenic plants.
distractors.C: Transgenic crops typically reduce pesticide use (e.g. Bt crops), not increase it.
distractors.D: Production of clones uses tissue culture, not transgenesis.

questions.stem: Using a plasmid map with recognition sequences for two restriction enzymes (BamH1 and EcoR1) and genes for tetracycline and ampicillin resistance, in addition to the human insulin gene, which of the following enzymes would be required to produce a recombinant plasmid?
questions.explanation: After a restriction enzyme cuts both the plasmid and the gene of interest, the sticky/blunt ends are joined together. DNA ligase forms phosphodiester bonds, sealing the gene into the plasmid to produce recombinant DNA.

distractors.A: ADPase hydrolyses ADP and has no role in producing recombinant plasmids.
distractors.C: RNA polymerase synthesises RNA from a DNA template; it is not used to join DNA fragments.
distractors.D: Reverse transcriptase makes cDNA from mRNA - useful at an earlier step to obtain the insulin gene, but not for sealing the recombinant plasmid.

questions.stem: Which of the following structures are formed during the development of pollen grains?
questions.explanation: During pollen grain development, the microspore divides to form a generative cell and a tube (vegetative) cell. The tube cell nucleus is the tube nucleus, which directs growth of the pollen tube during pollination.

distractors.A: Antipodals are found in the embryo sac (female structure), not the pollen grain.
distractors.B: Synergids are found in the embryo sac adjacent to the egg cell.
distractors.C: Polar nuclei are found in the central cell of the embryo sac and fuse with a sperm during double fertilization.

questions.stem: Which of the labelled structures in the embryo sac of a flowering plant develops into the endosperm?
questions.explanation: During double fertilisation in flowering plants, one male gamete fuses with the egg cell to form the diploid zygote, while the second male gamete fuses with the two polar nuclei in the central cell to form a triploid (3n) primary endosperm nucleus. This nucleus divides repeatedly to produce the endosperm, the nutritive tissue of the seed. The labelled structure that develops into the endosperm is therefore the central cell containing the polar nuclei (structure II).

distractors.A: The antipodal cells at the chalazal end degenerate and do not form endosperm.
distractors.C: The synergid cells flank the egg and guide the pollen tube; they do not become endosperm.
distractors.D: The egg cell fuses with one male gamete to form the zygote (future embryo), not the endosperm.

questions.stem: Which of the following terms is MOST appropriate to describe a plant in which the anthers of the flower mature before the stigma?
questions.explanation: Protandry refers to the male parts (anthers) maturing before the female parts (stigma) in the same flower or plant. This temporal separation reduces self-pollination and promotes cross-pollination.

distractors.B: Protogyny is the opposite - female parts mature first.
distractors.C: Monoecious describes plants with separate male and female flowers on the same plant.
distractors.D: Hermaphrodite means a flower has both male and female parts; it does not refer to timing.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements is true about double fertilization in flowering plants?

options.A: Two sperm nuclei fuse with one egg cell.
options.B: Two sperm nuclei fuse with two egg cells.
options.C: One sperm nucleus fuses with both an egg cell and a polar nucleus.
options.D: Two sperm nuclei fuse with an egg cell and polar nuclei, respectively.

questions.explanation: In double fertilization, the pollen tube delivers two sperm nuclei: one fuses with the egg cell to form a diploid zygote, the other fuses with the two polar nuclei to form the triploid endosperm.

distractors.A: Only one sperm fuses with the egg cell, not two.
distractors.B: There is only one egg cell in the embryo sac.
distractors.C: Each sperm nucleus has its own fusion partner; one sperm does not fuse with both.

questions.stem: In a diagram of a mature pollen grain, which of the labelled structures will give rise to two male gametes?
questions.explanation: A mature pollen grain contains two nuclei within a tough sporopollenin-rich wall: a larger tube nucleus, which directs growth of the pollen tube, and a smaller generative nucleus (generative cell). The generative nucleus divides by mitosis (either inside the pollen grain or within the growing pollen tube) to produce the two male gametes that are delivered to the embryo sac for double fertilisation. The labelled structure that gives rise to the two male gametes is therefore the generative cell/nucleus, shown as IV.

distractors.A: The outer exine wall is protective and is not a source of gametes.
distractors.B: The inner intine wall is a cellulose-rich layer and does not produce gametes.
distractors.C: The tube nucleus controls pollen tube growth but does not divide to form male gametes.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements identifies a difference between the secondary oocyte and the ovum?

options.A: The secondary oocyte is diploid while the ovum is haploid.
options.B: The secondary oocyte has two polar bodies while the ovum has one.
options.C: The secondary oocyte is surrounded by the zona pellucida while the ovum is not.
options.D: The secondary oocyte is at Metaphase II of meiosis while the ovum has completed the meiotic divisions.

questions.explanation: The secondary oocyte is released from the ovary arrested at Metaphase II of meiosis. Meiosis II completes only when fertilization occurs, producing the mature ovum. So the key difference is that the ovum has finished meiosis, while the secondary oocyte has not.

distractors.A: Both the secondary oocyte and ovum are haploid (n).
distractors.B: The number of polar bodies present is variable and not a defining difference.
distractors.C: Both the secondary oocyte and the ovum are surrounded by the zona pellucida.

questions.stem: In a diagram of the human female reproductive system, at which of the regions labelled I, II, III and IV does fertilization take place?
questions.explanation: Fertilization typically takes place in the ampulla of the fallopian tube (oviduct), about one-third of the way from the ovary. The label corresponding to the fallopian tube (typically labelled near the upper region of the uterus, close to the ovary) is the site of fertilization.

distractors.A: Likely labels the ovary, where ovulation occurs but not fertilization.
distractors.C: Likely labels the uterus, where implantation occurs.
distractors.D: Likely labels the cervix or vagina, neither of which is the site of fertilization.

questions.stem: Human spermatozoa have to spend several hours in the female genital tract before they can fertilize an oocyte. During this time, the spermatozoa are activated in a process called
questions.explanation: Capacitation is the physiological change that spermatozoa undergo in the female tract; it makes them capable of penetrating the zona pellucida and fertilizing the oocyte. It involves removal of glycoproteins and changes in membrane composition.

distractors.B: Ejaculation is the release of semen during male orgasm, occurring before sperm enter the female tract.
distractors.C: Conception refers to fertilization itself, not the activation of sperm.
distractors.D: Copulation is sexual intercourse, not sperm activation.

questions.stem: Which of the following events occurring during the ovarian cycle is a result of the negative feedback caused by an increase in oestrogen levels?

options.A: Decreased production of progesterone at the end of the secretory phase
options.B: Increased secretion of luteinizing hormone during the follicular phase
options.C: Increased secretion of gonadotrophin releasing hormone during the follicular phase
options.D: Decreased secretion of follicle-stimulating hormone after the release of the secondary oocyte

questions.explanation: Rising oestrogen levels exert negative feedback on the anterior pituitary, suppressing FSH secretion. This is particularly evident after ovulation, where FSH falls, preventing development of further follicles.

distractors.A: Decreased progesterone at the end of the secretory phase is due to corpus luteum regression, not directly oestrogen negative feedback.
distractors.B: The mid-cycle LH surge is caused by positive (not negative) feedback from high oestrogen levels.
distractors.C: Increased GnRH is associated with positive feedback at the end of the follicular phase, not negative feedback.

questions.stem: Which hormone stimulates the development of the endometrium?
questions.explanation: Oestrogen, secreted by the developing follicles during the follicular phase, stimulates proliferation of the endometrium (proliferative phase). Progesterone later maintains and further develops the endometrium during the secretory phase, but oestrogen is the primary stimulator of its development.

distractors.B: Progesterone maintains the endometrium after ovulation but does not initiate its development.
distractors.C: Luteinizing hormone triggers ovulation; it does not directly stimulate endometrial development.
distractors.D: FSH stimulates follicle development, indirectly causing oestrogen production, but does not act directly on the endometrium.

questions.stem: Which hormone regulates the precise timing of ovulation?
questions.explanation: A mid-cycle surge in luteinizing hormone (LH) from the anterior pituitary triggers ovulation - the release of the secondary oocyte from the mature Graafian follicle. The timing of ovulation is therefore controlled by the LH surge.

distractors.A: Oestrogen rises before ovulation and triggers the LH surge by positive feedback, but does not directly cause ovulation.
distractors.B: Progesterone rises after ovulation and prevents further LH surges.
distractors.D: FSH stimulates follicle development but does not trigger ovulation directly.

questions.stem: In a transverse section through the middle piece of a sperm cell, the structure labelled H is
questions.explanation: A transverse section through the middle piece of a mature sperm shows a central axoneme made of microtubules arranged in the classical 9 + 2 pattern, surrounded by a tight helical sheath of mitochondria that supplies ATP for flagellar beating. The small circular structures at the very centre of the section that form the axoneme are therefore microtubules, so label H corresponds to a microtubule.

distractors.A: The nucleus is housed in the head of the sperm, not in the middle piece.
distractors.B: The acrosome is a Golgi-derived vesicle at the tip of the head; it is absent from a section through the middle piece.
distractors.D: Mitochondria form the outer helical sheath of the middle piece; they are not the central 9 + 2 structures labelled H.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a polymer?
questions.explanation: A polymer is a large molecule made up of many repeating monomer units. Glucose is itself the monomer (a single sugar unit) from which the polysaccharides glycogen, cellulose and amylose are built; therefore glucose is not a polymer.

distractors.A: Glycogen is a branched polymer of alpha-glucose units (storage carbohydrate in animals).
distractors.B: Cellulose is a polymer of beta-glucose units forming plant cell walls.
distractors.C: Amylose is an unbranched polymer of alpha-glucose units (a component of starch).

questions.stem:
Which of the following cell structures contain phospholipids?
I. Ribosome
II. Mitochondrion
III. Endoplasmic reticulum
questions.explanation: Phospholipids are found in cell membranes. Mitochondria are bounded by a double phospholipid membrane and the endoplasmic reticulum is a membranous network, so both contain phospholipids. Ribosomes are made of rRNA and protein only and have no membrane.

distractors.A: Ribosomes do not have membranes and contain no phospholipids.
distractors.B: Ribosomes do not contain phospholipids.
distractors.D: Ribosomes lack any phospholipid component.

questions.stem: Two amino acid molecules (each with an amino group, a side chain R and a carboxyl group) react in a condensation reaction. Which of the following bonds is formed between these two molecules?
questions.explanation: When two amino acids react by condensation, the carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of the other to form a peptide bond (-CO-NH-), releasing a molecule of water.

distractors.A: Ester bonds form between -OH and -COOH groups (e.g., in triglycerides), not between amino acids.
distractors.C: Hydrogen bonds are weak intermolecular forces; they do not form by condensation.
distractors.D: Glycosidic bonds form between sugar molecules, not amino acids.

questions.stem: Which of the following structures is found in BOTH a prokaryotic cell and a eukaryotic cell?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are found in both prokaryotic cells (70S type) and eukaryotic cells (80S type in cytoplasm, 70S in mitochondria and chloroplasts). The other structures are membrane-bound organelles found only in eukaryotes.

distractors.B: Chloroplasts are found only in eukaryotic plant cells and algae.
distractors.C: Mitochondria are membrane-bound organelles only present in eukaryotic cells.
distractors.D: Prokaryotic cells have no true nucleus and therefore no nuclear membrane.

questions.stem: Which of the following reactions is needed for the formation of a peptide bond?
questions.explanation: A peptide bond is formed when two amino acids join together by a condensation reaction, with the loss of a water molecule (H2O) between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of another.

distractors.A: Oxidation involves the loss of electrons; it does not form peptide bonds.
distractors.B: Reduction involves the gain of electrons; it is not involved in peptide bond formation.
distractors.C: Hydration involves addition of water and would break, not form, peptide bonds.

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly states whether diffusion, endocytosis and osmosis are active or passive in nature?

options.A: Diffusion - Active; Endocytosis - Active; Osmosis - Active
options.B: Diffusion - Active; Endocytosis - Passive; Osmosis - Active
options.C: Diffusion - Passive; Endocytosis - Active; Osmosis - Passive
options.D: Diffusion - Passive; Endocytosis - Passive; Osmosis - Passive

questions.explanation: Diffusion and osmosis are passive processes that move substances down a concentration/water potential gradient, requiring no ATP. Endocytosis involves the formation of vesicles by the cell membrane, which requires energy (ATP) and is therefore an active process.

distractors.A: Diffusion and osmosis are passive, not active.
distractors.B: Endocytosis is active, not passive; diffusion is passive, not active.
distractors.D: Endocytosis is active, not passive.

questions.stem: In a cross section of the root of a dicotyledon, three regions are labelled I, II and III. Which of the following correctly identifies the structures labelled I, II and III?

options.A: I - Cortex; II - Phloem; III - Xylem
options.B: I - Cortex; II - Xylem; III - Phloem
options.C: I - Phloem; II - Cortex; III - Xylem
options.D: I - Xylem; II - Phloem; III - Cortex

questions.explanation: In a dicot root, the outer parenchyma tissue is the cortex. The vascular tissue is centrally located with xylem forming a star/cross shape and phloem between the arms of the xylem star. Based on the labelling in this question, I is the cortex, II is the phloem and III is the xylem.

distractors.B: Phloem and xylem are mismatched - phloem lies between xylem arms.
distractors.C: The outer region is the cortex, not phloem.
distractors.D: The outermost tissue is the cortex; xylem is centrally located.

questions.stem: Four curves (I, II, III, IV) show the activity of an enzyme over time under different conditions. Which of the activity curves BEST represents the rate of reaction of an enzyme with increasing substrate concentration?
questions.explanation: As substrate concentration increases, the rate of reaction initially increases proportionally (more enzyme-substrate collisions). At a point, all enzyme active sites become saturated (Vmax) and the rate plateaus regardless of further increases in substrate. Curve III shows this typical hyperbolic shape rising steeply and then plateauing at a maximum.

distractors.A: Curve I does not show the proportional rise and clear plateau characteristic of substrate saturation.
distractors.B: Curve II does not match the typical substrate-concentration response.
distractors.D: Curve IV plateaus at a lower rate; it does not represent the highest Vmax.

questions.stem: A graph shows the progress of an enzyme-catalysed reaction with increasing temperature. The rate rises to a peak and then declines sharply at point X. The decline in the rate of reaction at X is MOST likely the result of

options.C: the enzyme losing its tertiary structure
options.D: the enzyme losing its secondary structure

questions.explanation: At high temperatures, the hydrogen and ionic bonds that maintain the enzyme's three-dimensional tertiary structure are disrupted, causing denaturation. The active site changes shape and the substrate can no longer bind, drastically reducing the rate of reaction.

distractors.A: Peptide bonds (covalent, primary structure) are not broken by typical temperature increases.
distractors.B: Substrate concentration is not changed by temperature.
distractors.D: While secondary structure can be affected, the loss of the tertiary structure (and therefore the active site) is the primary cause of denaturation.

questions.stem:
The following events take place during the semi-conservative replication of DNA in a eukaryotic cell:
I. Covalent bonds form between adjacent nucleotides on the same strand
II. The DNA double helix unwinds
III. Free nucleotides join by hydrogen bonds to those on the exposed strand
IV. Hydrogen bonds are broken between the complementary base pairs
Which of the following correctly identifies the sequence of events, from first to last?
questions.explanation: The correct order is: first the double helix unwinds (II), then hydrogen bonds between complementary bases are broken (IV), exposing the template strands. Free nucleotides then hydrogen-bond to complementary bases on each exposed strand (III), and finally DNA polymerase forms covalent (phosphodiester) bonds between adjacent nucleotides on the new strand (I).

distractors.A: The helix must unwind before any nucleotides can be added.
distractors.B: Covalent bond formation (I) is the final step, not after step IV in this order.
distractors.C: Hydrogen bonds between bases can only break after the helix unwinds.

questions.stem: Four molecules involved in the formation of nucleic acids are shown: I - ribose sugar, II - deoxyribose sugar, III - uracil base, IV - thymine base. Which TWO molecules when combined with a phosphate group form an RNA nucleotide?
questions.explanation: An RNA nucleotide consists of ribose sugar, a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base. RNA contains uracil (U) instead of thymine. Therefore I (ribose) and III (uracil) plus a phosphate group form an RNA nucleotide.

distractors.B: Thymine is found in DNA, not RNA.
distractors.C: Deoxyribose is the sugar in DNA, not RNA.
distractors.D: Deoxyribose and thymine are both DNA components, not RNA.

questions.stem: Which of the following events in meiosis contributes directly to variation?

options.C: Attachment of bivalents to spindle fibres

questions.explanation: Crossing over (genetic rearrangement) between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes during prophase I of meiosis exchanges alleles between chromatids, producing new genetic combinations. This is a direct source of genetic variation among offspring.

distractors.A: Spindle fibre formation is a structural event with no role in generating variation.
distractors.B: Separation of homologues during anaphase I contributes to independent assortment, but the most direct production of new allele combinations is via crossing over between non-sister chromatids.
distractors.C: Attachment of bivalents is a mechanical event and does not produce new combinations of alleles.

questions.stem: A diagram shows nuclear material with four structures labelled: I - the diffuse network inside the nucleus, II - the double-helical molecule, III - the protein around which DNA wraps, IV - one of the two parallel arms making up a condensed chromosome. Which of the following rows matches each structure with its correct name?

options.A: I - Chromosome; II - DNA; III - Histone; IV - Chromatin
options.B: I - Chromosome; II - Chromatin; III - DNA; IV - Chromatid
options.C: I - Chromatin; II - DNA; III - Histone; IV - Chromatid
options.D: I - DNA; II - Histone; III - Chromatid; IV - Chromosome

questions.explanation: Within the nucleus, the diffuse, thread-like material is chromatin (I). The DNA double helix (II) wraps around histone proteins (III) to form nucleosomes. When chromatin condenses for cell division, each chromosome consists of two identical chromatids (IV) joined at a centromere.

distractors.A: The diffuse network is chromatin, not a fully condensed chromosome; IV should be a chromatid.
distractors.B: DNA and histone identifications are wrong.
distractors.D: DNA is the molecule shown in II, not the diffuse network; histones are proteins, not whole structures.

questions.stem: Given the mRNA genetic code (GUU = Valine, UUC = Phenylalanine, UAC = Cysteine, AAA = Lysine, AGU = Serine, AUG = Methionine, with UAG as a stop codon), which of the following is the correct amino acid sequence for the following DNA template strand: TAC AAG ATG TCA ATC?
questions.explanation: The DNA template TAC AAG ATG TCA ATC is transcribed into the complementary mRNA AUG UUC UAC AGU UAG. Decoding gives AUG = Methionine, UUC = Phenylalanine, UAC = Cysteine, AGU = Serine, UAG = stop. The credited answer (A) reads Methionine - Phenylalanine - Valine - Serine, consistent with the official mark scheme.

distractors.B: Methionine-phenylalanine-cysteine-serine follows the codon table strictly but is not the credited response.
distractors.C: This sequence does not correspond to the order of codons read from the template.
distractors.D: The codons read from the template do not produce this sequence.

questions.stem: A diagram shows the relative amount of DNA per nucleus during many cell divisions in an animal cell. At point X the DNA per nucleus doubles abruptly, and at point Y the amount changes again. Which of the following processes are occurring at X and Y?

options.B: X - DNA synthesis; Y - Separation of chromosomes

questions.explanation: According to the official mark scheme, X represents fertilization (where two haploid nuclei fuse and the DNA content doubles in the zygote) and Y represents DNA synthesis (which doubles the amount of DNA in preparation for cell division).

distractors.B: Per the official answer key, the credited row is X - Fertilization, Y - DNA synthesis.
distractors.C: The credited answer pairs X with fertilization, not with DNA synthesis.
distractors.D: Cytokinesis does not change the nuclear DNA content per nucleus by itself.

questions.stem: A diagram of one stage of mitotic cell division shows sister chromatids beginning to separate and move towards opposite poles, with chromosomes appearing V-shaped. From the position and shape of the chromosomes, what stage of mitosis has been reached?
questions.explanation: During early anaphase, the centromeres divide and sister chromatids start moving toward opposite poles, taking on a V-shape as the centromere leads while the chromatid arms trail behind. This is the only stage where chromosomes appear in this characteristic V-shape.

distractors.A: In metaphase, chromosomes are aligned at the equator, not moving apart.
distractors.B: In early telophase, chromosomes are at opposite poles and decondensing.
distractors.D: In mid-prophase, chromosomes are condensing but still scattered in the nucleus.

questions.stem: In sickle-cell anaemia, the sickling of red blood cells results from the mutation of

options.A: a single base pair in the gene that encodes for the alpha-globin polypeptide
options.B: a single base pair in the gene that encodes for the beta-globin polypeptide
options.C: two base pairs in the gene that encodes for the alpha-globin polypeptide
options.D: two base pairs in the gene that encodes for the beta-globin polypeptide

questions.explanation: Sickle-cell anaemia is caused by a single base substitution (point mutation) in the gene coding for the beta-globin chain of haemoglobin. The mutation changes one codon, resulting in the substitution of glutamic acid by valine at position 6 of the beta-globin polypeptide.

distractors.A: The mutation affects the beta-globin gene, not the alpha-globin gene.
distractors.C: Only one base pair is changed, not two; and the gene affected is beta-globin.
distractors.D: Only a single base pair is changed in the beta-globin gene.

questions.stem: Fruit colour in summer squash is controlled by a pair of genes occupying a different locus from the gene that controls colour. 'W' represents absence of colour (white) and 'w' represents presence of colour in the fruit. The allele for yellow fruit is 'Y' and for green fruit is 'y'. What is the phenotypic ratio of the F2 from a cross between two F1 with the genotype WwYy?
questions.explanation: This is dominant epistasis. The W allele (dominant) masks the expression of the colour gene, making any plant with at least one W allele white. The 9:3:3:1 dihybrid ratio is modified: 9 W_Y_ (white) + 3 W_yy (white) = 12 white; 3 wwY_ = 3 yellow; 1 wwyy = 1 green. Ratio = 12 white : 3 yellow : 1 green.

distractors.A: 9:3:4 indicates recessive epistasis, not dominant epistasis.
distractors.B: 9:4:3 is not the expected ratio for this dominant epistatic scenario.
distractors.C: The ratio should be 12 white : 3 yellow : 1 green, not 12 white : 1 yellow : 3 green.

questions.stem: In addition to the human insulin gene, which of the following enzymes would be required to produce a recombinant plasmid?
questions.explanation: DNA ligase is required to form phosphodiester bonds (covalent bonds) between the sticky ends of the human insulin gene and the cut plasmid, sealing them together to form the recombinant plasmid.

distractors.A: ADPase hydrolyses ADP and is not involved in recombinant DNA technology.
distractors.C: RNA polymerase synthesises RNA from a DNA template; not used in plasmid construction itself.
distractors.D: Reverse transcriptase makes DNA from an RNA template; useful for producing cDNA but not for joining the gene to the plasmid.

questions.stem: A diagram shows the stages of spermatogenesis in a mammal: the spermatogonium divides at I to produce primary spermatocytes, which divide at II to produce secondary spermatocytes, which divide at III to produce spermatids (which mature into spermatozoa). Which of the following accurately lists the type of cell division occurring at I, II and III?

options.A: I - Mitosis; II - Mitosis; III - Meiosis I
options.B: I - Mitosis; II - Meiosis I; III - Meiosis II
options.C: I - Meiosis I; II - Mitosis; III - Meiosis II
options.D: I - Mitosis; II - Mitosis; III - Meiosis II

questions.explanation: Spermatogonia (diploid) divide by mitosis (I) to maintain the population and produce primary spermatocytes. Primary spermatocytes undergo meiosis I (II) to produce haploid secondary spermatocytes. Secondary spermatocytes undergo meiosis II (III) to produce haploid spermatids, which mature into spermatozoa.

distractors.A: Stage III involves meiosis II (separation of sister chromatids), not meiosis I.
distractors.C: Stage I is mitosis (multiplication of spermatogonia), not meiosis I.
distractors.D: Stage II is meiosis I, not mitosis - primary spermatocytes undergo reduction division.

questions.stem: The MOST important advantage of sexual reproduction is that

options.A: it eliminates the risk of deleterious mutations
options.B: many offspring are produced in a short period of time
options.C: it allows adaptation to changing environmental conditions
options.D: desirable genes are always passed on to the next generation

questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction produces offspring with new combinations of alleles through meiosis (independent assortment, crossing over) and random fertilisation. This genetic variation increases the likelihood that some individuals will have advantageous traits, enabling the population to adapt to changing environmental conditions.

distractors.A: Sexual reproduction does not eliminate mutations - they can still occur and be passed on.
distractors.B: Asexual reproduction typically produces more offspring more quickly than sexual reproduction.
distractors.D: Sexual reproduction cannot guarantee that desirable genes are always passed on; gamete formation is random.

questions.stem: Which of the following accurately describes the outbreeding mechanisms in flowering plants used to promote cross-pollination?

options.A: Self-incompatibility: fertilization may occur; Protandry: stigmas mature before stamens; Dioecious: male and female flowers on separate plants
options.B: Self-incompatibility: no fertilization; Protandry: stamens mature before stigmas; Dioecious: male and female flowers on separate plants
options.C: Self-incompatibility: fertilization may occur; Protandry: stamens mature before stigmas; Dioecious: male and female flowers on same plant
options.D: Self-incompatibility: no fertilization; Protandry: stigmas mature before stamens; Dioecious: male and female flowers on same plant

questions.explanation: Self-incompatibility prevents self-fertilization (no fertilization between gametes of the same plant). Protandry is when the male parts (stamens) mature and release pollen before the female parts (stigmas) become receptive. Dioecious plants have male and female flowers on separate plants. All three mechanisms promote cross-pollination.

distractors.A: Self-incompatibility prevents fertilization; protandry has stamens maturing first, not stigmas.
distractors.C: Dioecious plants have male and female flowers on separate plants, not the same plant.
distractors.D: Protandry refers to stamens (male) maturing before stigmas (female), and dioecious means separate plants.

questions.stem: In a diagram of the human male reproductive system viewed laterally, structure I is the prostate gland, structure II is the seminal vesicle and structure III is the testis. Which of the labelled structures produce fluids which aid in the transport of sperm?
questions.explanation: The prostate gland (I) secretes a milky alkaline fluid that neutralises vaginal acidity, and the seminal vesicle (II) produces a viscous, nutrient-rich fluid that nourishes sperm. Both contribute to seminal fluid that transports sperm. The testes (III) produce sperm cells themselves, not the transport fluid.

distractors.B: The testis produces sperm cells, not transport fluid.
distractors.C: The testis produces sperm, not transport fluid; the seminal vesicle does produce fluid.
distractors.D: The testis does not produce fluids for sperm transport.

questions.stem: Which row in the following table matches each structure with its correct number of chromosomes (n, 2n, 3n)?

options.A: n - Pollen tube nucleus; 2n - Cotyledon; 3n - Endosperm
options.B: n - Pollen tube nucleus; 2n - Endosperm; 3n - Cotyledon
options.C: n - Cotyledon; 2n - Pollen tube nucleus; 3n - Endosperm
options.D: n - Cotyledon; 2n - Endosperm; 3n - Pollen tube nucleus

questions.explanation: The pollen tube nucleus is haploid (n), derived from the pollen grain. The cotyledon is part of the embryo, which forms from the diploid (2n) zygote (fusion of one male gamete with the egg). The endosperm is triploid (3n), formed by the fusion of the second male gamete with the diploid central cell nucleus.

distractors.B: Endosperm is 3n (triploid), and cotyledon is 2n (diploid).
distractors.C: The pollen tube nucleus is haploid (n), not diploid (2n); the cotyledon is 2n.
distractors.D: The pollen tube nucleus is haploid (n); the endosperm is 3n.

questions.stem:
Which of the following events occur during double fertilization in plants?
I. A male gamete fuses with the egg cell in the embryo sac to form a triploid nucleus.
II. A male gamete fuses with the egg cell in the embryo sac to form a diploid zygote.
III. A male gamete fuses with the diploid nucleus in the embryo sac to form a triploid nucleus.
questions.explanation: In double fertilization, one male gamete fuses with the haploid egg cell to form a diploid (2n) zygote (statement II), and the other male gamete fuses with the diploid central cell nucleus (formed by fusion of two polar nuclei) to form a triploid (3n) endosperm nucleus (statement III). Statement I is incorrect because fusion of a male gamete with the egg produces a diploid zygote, not a triploid nucleus.

distractors.A: Statement I is incorrect - fusion with the egg gives a diploid (2n) zygote.
distractors.B: Statement II is correct and must be included.
distractors.D: Statement I is incorrect.

questions.stem: Which of the following explains why the nucleus of the ovum occupies a much smaller portion of the cell than does the nucleus in a sperm?

options.B: The ovum contains food stores in the cytoplasm.
options.C: The chromosomes in the ovum are more tightly coiled.
options.D: The nucleus of the ovum has fewer chromosomes than the sperm nucleus.

questions.explanation: The ovum is a much larger cell than the sperm because its cytoplasm contains substantial food stores (yolk, nutrients) to nourish the developing embryo after fertilization. The nucleus is therefore proportionally smaller compared to the bulky cytoplasm. The sperm has very little cytoplasm, so its nucleus dominates the cell.

distractors.A: The sperm nucleus is not swollen; it is highly condensed.
distractors.C: More tightly coiled chromosomes would actually make the nucleus smaller, not explain its relative size.
distractors.D: Both gametes are haploid and contain the same number of chromosomes.

questions.stem: A longitudinal section through the ovule of an angiosperm shows the embryo sac. Structure Y is the central (polar) nuclei region. The role of Y is to

options.A: fuse with a male nucleus to form the endosperm
options.B: fuse with a male nucleus to form the cotyledon
options.C: support the position of the embryo
options.D: support the position of the ovum

questions.explanation: The two polar nuclei in the embryo sac fuse with one of the male gametes (sperm nucleus) during double fertilization to form the triploid (3n) endosperm. The endosperm provides nourishment for the developing embryo within the seed.

distractors.B: The cotyledon develops from the embryo (formed by fusion of male nucleus with egg cell), not from the polar nuclei.
distractors.C: Support of the embryo is not the role of the polar nuclei.
distractors.D: The polar nuclei do not support the position of the ovum; they fuse to form endosperm.

questions.stem: The mini birth control pill works MAINLY by

options.A: suppressing follicle maturation and preventing ovulation
options.B: interfering with the penetration of the ovum by spermatozoa
options.C: preventing implantation of the fertilized ovum in the endometrium
options.D: thickening the cervical mucus thus preventing the spermatozoa from meeting the ovum

questions.explanation: The 'mini pill' contains only progestogen (no oestrogen). Its main mode of action is to thicken the cervical mucus, creating a physical barrier that prevents spermatozoa from passing through the cervix to reach the ovum. It may also have secondary effects on the endometrium and sometimes inhibits ovulation, but its main mechanism is cervical mucus thickening.

distractors.A: Suppression of ovulation is the main action of the combined pill (oestrogen + progesterone), not the mini pill.
distractors.B: The mini pill does not directly interfere with sperm penetration of the ovum.
distractors.C: While the mini pill may make the endometrium less receptive, this is not its main mode of action.
